event: Delivered by emergency cesarean delivery at 31 weeks of pregnancy
Apgar values of 4 and 8 in the first and fifth minutes of life
respiration rate of 65 breaths per min and chest retraction
did not exhibit any signs of dysmorphia
blood count, electrolytes, and liver function were all normal when he was admitted
Respiratory distress syndrome ground-glass opacity was seen on the X-ray
minor respiratory acidosis was detected in the patient’s blood gas
Acute respiratory distress syndrome had been diagnosed
a raised C-reactive protein level of 70 mg/L
antibiotics were escalated to meropenem and vancomycin
feeding was restarted at a rate of 30 mL/kg/day
septic examination results were always negative
brain magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) were normal
nasogastric tube was eventually able to provide around half of his nutritional requirements
He did not have pale stools
the neonatologist recommended a neonatal cholestasis workup
suspected a relationship between hepatitis and total parenteral nutrition
there was no change in cholestasis
genetic studies on neonatal cholestasis were prompted
no evidence of intrahepatic or extrahepatic biliary tree dilatation
albumin level began to decline over time
Bilirubin, AST, and ALT levels rose
an infectious etiology investigation was conducted
the levels of TSH, T3, T4, and cortisol were not significant
he tested negative for a wide range of metabolic disorders
caffeine (5 mg/kg/day) and hemoglobin optimization failed to manage recurrent apnea and bradycardia
phenobarbitone was used as an empirical treatment
remained severely sick, with a high C-reactive protein level of 223.3 mg/L
The patient died after 100 days in the hospital
An autosomal dominant ALGS2 was caused by a pathogenic heterozygous variant c.1076c>T (Ser359Phe) chr1: 120512166 in the NOTCH2 gene
